ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn class for chimichanga exists; 'burrito (food product)' FOODON_03311069 is the closest parent class. Chimichanga is a deep-fried variant of a burrito.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FMT (Chicken dishes) used as the closest defensible category anchor. No specific chimichanga code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chimichanga or equivalent fried burrito dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately one chimichanga weighing around 170 g, as commonly served in restaurant or cafeteria settings.

nutrient_source.title: Chimichanga, chicken and cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 304 kcal, protein 12.5 g, fat 16.42 g, carbohydrate 25.76 g, sugar 1.96 g, sodium 414 mg, dietary fibre 1.8 g, potassium 147 mg, calcium 107 mg, magnesium 20 mg, iron 2.11 mg, cholesterol 36 mg) are sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95) as provided in the input record. HPB is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat estimated at 5.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken and cheese chimichanga (NDB No. 21066 / closest SR Legacy match); monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported in HPB for this dish.

gi_source.title: Burrito with chicken and cheese — analogous GI estimate based on flour tortilla-wrapped mixed dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken chimichanga exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the closest analogous entry: flour tortilla-based mixed dish with protein and fat (GI ~55–60 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat flour tortilla wraps and mixed chicken-cheese burritos). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (43.8 g for 170 g serving) / 100 = 25.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 414 mg/100g with low potassium offset (147 mg/100g), yielding an unfavourable Na:K ratio of approximately 2.8:1
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid chicken chimichangas due to the high sodium content (414 mg/100g) and poor potassium-to-sodium ratio; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 100 g and should be paired with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or tomatoes to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate load 25.76 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) and medium GI (~58), producing a high glycaemic load per serving (~25.4)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit chicken chimichangas because the high glycaemic load (~25.4 per serving) and low fibre content (1.8 g/100g) will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting a whole-wheat tortilla and baking instead of deep-frying can reduce the glycaemic impact and eliminate trans fat that worsens insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~5.8 g/100g, cholesterol 36 mg/100g, sodium 414 mg/100g, and deep-frying process generating pro-atherogenic lipid oxidation products
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried chicken chimichangas due to the combination of elevated saturated fat (~5.8 g/100g), cholesterol (36 mg/100g), and high sodium (414 mg/100g), all of which contribute to LDL elevation, arterial stiffness, and blood pressure burden; a baked version with reduced cheese and low-sodium seasoning would substantially lower cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids that promote joint inflammation; saturated fat ~5.8 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chimichangas as the AGEs generated during frying and the elevated saturated fat content (~5.8 g/100g) are pro-inflammatory; baking the chimichanga and reducing cheese content would lower both AGE formation and saturated fat to below the 5 g/100g RA threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken chimichangas are a Tex-Mex / Mexican-American dish consisting of a flour tortilla filled with seasoned shredded or diced chicken, melted cheese, and spices, then folded into a rectangular parcel and deep-fried until the exterior is golden-brown and crispy. The dish originated in the American Southwest and is widely served in Tex-Mex restaurants globally, including in Singapore. The texture contrasts a crunchy, blistered tortilla shell with a moist, savoury chicken-and-cheese interior. Per 100 g, chicken chimichangas provide 304 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 16.42 g fat (of which approximately 5.8 g is saturated), and 25.76 g carbohydrate with only 1.8 g dietary fibre, making them a calorie-dense, moderate-protein, high-fat dish. Sodium is elevated at 414 mg/100g. The deep-frying process increases caloric density and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids. Clinically, the dish poses concerns for individuals with hypertension (high sodium, poor Na:K ratio), Type 2 Diabetes (high glycaemic load ~25.4 per serving, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, cholesterol, sodium). Healthier preparation alternatives include baking instead of deep-frying and using whole-wheat tortillas to increase fibre content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken chimichanga?
What is the difference between a burrito and a chimichanga?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (approved primary regional source, confidence 0.95 per input record). Saturated fat estimated from the closest USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy match for chicken-and-cheese chimichanga. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for flour tortilla-based mixed dishes; no direct GI measurement for chimichanga exists in the approved GI database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.

GI of 58 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for chicken chimichangas is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. This dish is classified as Western / Tex-Mex cuisine; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null as this is not a Singaporean dish. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
